text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'll walk through a realworld use case of crafting an ideal customer profile using AI. So before talking about any of the technical AI stuff, let's just start with the business side of this use case and answer the question, what is an ICP? ICP is short for ideal customer profile and it's simply this idea that we might have a diverse range of customers that pay for our product or service. But there exists a subset of these customers that make up our best customer. Best in this case can mean many different things. Maybe they're the best because they have the greatest lifetime value. They might be the best because you really enjoy working with them. They might be the best because they always leave five-star reviews or they give you a lot of referrals. How you define best will depend on the business context. But some things you might include in an ICP are things like the role that the customer has, the company size, goals, pain points, psychoraphics, demographics, and so on and so forth. There doesn't seem to be a single formula that this is all the boxes that you need to fill in for a ideal customer profile. But these are just some examples of things you might include. An ideal customer profile is just a description of our quote unquote best customer. But why should I care about this? Why is this important? This is actually something I've learned the hard way because when I first launched my AI cohorts, I didn't really have a specific customer profile in mind. I was just saying, "Hey, this is a six week cohort on AI and you can take it and we'll build projects and things like that." And it wasn't really targeted toward anyone. And this turned out to make my life a lot harder when it came to actually marketing and selling the course. Having a very clear idea of your ideal customer profile is going to be the foundation for the positioning, the marketing, and the messaging of your product or service. So, an analogy I like to think about here is that when you're trying to sell to multiple customers, your marketing and sales efforts are kind of like a dull arrowhead. This might make an impact and get some people's attention, but it's not going to be as effective as an arrow head that looks like this. And so this is what your marketing and sales efforts look like when you try to reach only a single customer profile. The second way is to have an LLM with tools classify it. So this is better for like hard categories. And an important note here is that using the embeddings directly is going to be hard to interpret down the line. But by putting them into clusters, we actually have a chance at interpreting them because we can just look at that specific cluster. Like we looked specifically at cluster 4 and then we can also give all the clusters to GPT to do some kind of analysis to compare the cluster that we care about to all the other ones. The second takeaway is that there's still a place for traditional machine learning approaches today. Everyone's so focused on large language models and people just try to do everything with LLMs, but they're not so great at working with numbers. And when you have some clear KPIs that you're trying to optimize for, like someone leaving a fivestar review, you probably don't want just a qualitative analysis. You want a more quantitative analysis, which is what you can get from techniques like logistic regression and other traditional machine learning approaches. And finally, the ICP is going to be really good context for an LLM when having it do copywriting, writing newsletters, doing LinkedIn posts, or whatever it might be.
